request.question: Why was O.J Mayo, named the Most Valuable Player at the 2007 Anaheim Classic dismissed from the NBA?

title: Scott Niedermayer
Jerry Lucas
Simona Gioli
Russell Westbrook
2007 Anaheim Classic
2013 NBA Summer League
Leanne Del Toso
Kevin Durant
CBA Most Valuable Player
O. J. Mayo
sentences: Scott Niedermayer (born August 31, 1973) is a Canadian former ice hockey defenceman and current Special Assignment Coach of the Anaheim Ducks.
He played 18 seasons and over 1,000 games in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the New Jersey Devils and Anaheim Ducks.
Niedermayer is a four-time Stanley Cup champion and played in five NHL All-Star Games.
He won the James Norris Memorial Trophy in 2003–04 as the NHL's top defenceman and the Conn Smythe Trophy in 2007 as the most valuable player of the playoffs.
On January 27, 2017, in a ceremony during the All-Star Weekend in Los Angeles, Niedermayer was part of the second group of players to be named one of the '100 Greatest NHL Players' in history.
Jerry Ray Lucas (born March 30, 1940) is an American former basketball player and memory education expert.

He was a nationally-awarded high school player, national college star at Ohio State, and 1960 gold medal Olympian and international player before starring as a professional player in the National Basketball Association (NBA).
As a collegian, Lucas led the Ohio State Buckeyes to the 1960 college national championship and three straight NCAA finals.
He remains today the only three-time Big Ten Player of the Year, and was also twice named NCAA Player of the Year.

As a professional, Lucas was named All-NBA First Team three times, a NBA All-Star seven times, was 1964 NBA Rookie of the Year, and was named Most Valuable Player of the 1965 NBA All-Star Game among other honors and awards.
He was inducted to the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame in 1980.
Simona Gioli (born September 17, 1977 in Rapallo) is a volleyball player from Italy.
Currently, she plays for Galatasaray in Turkey.
Gioli was a member of the Women's National Team that won the gold medal at the 2007 European Championship in Belgium and Luxembourg.
Gioli was named "Most Valuable Player and ""Best Blocker" at the 2007 FIVB Women's World Cup and 2009 FIVB Women's World Grand Champions Cup.
She was also awarded "MVP" at the 2006-07 CEV Cup won by her team Sirio Perugia.
She won the 2007–08 CEV Indesit Champions League with Sirio Perugia and also was individually awarded "Most Valuable Player".
Russell Westbrook III (born November 12, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
He is a six-time NBA All-Star, and a two-time NBA All-Star Game Most Valuable Player, winning consecutive awards in 2015 and 2016.
He is also a six-time All-NBA Team member and led the league in scoring in 2014–15 and 2016–17.
In 2017, Westbrook became one of two players in NBA history to average a triple-double for a season, along with Oscar Robertson in 1962.
He also set a record for the most triple-doubles in a season, with 42.
He was subsequently named the 2016–17 NBA Most Valuable Player.
The 2007 Anaheim Classic was played between November 22 and November 25, 2007 at the Anaheim Convention Center in Anaheim, California.
The champion of the tournament was Southern California, who defeated Southern Illinois in the Championship Game.
The Most Valuable Player was O. J. Mayo of Southern California.
The 2013 NBA Summer League is a pro basketball league run by the NBA just after the 2013 NBA draft.
It gives newly drafted players a chance to test their skills against each other, and to give them a feel for professional basketball.
All 30 NBA teams participated, along with the D-League Select.
The Miami Heat were the only team to participate in both Summer Leagues.
It ran from July 7–12 in Orlando and July 12–22 in Las Vegas.
Jeremy Lamb of the Oklahoma City Thunder was named the Most Valuable Player of the Orlando Summer League.
Jonas Valančiūnas of the Toronto Raptors went on to be named the Most Valuable Player of the Las Vegas Summer League.
Ian Clark of the Golden State Warriors was named the Most Valuable Player of the Las Vegas Summer League Championship Game.
Leanne Del Toso (born 12 August 1980) is a 3.5 point wheelchair basketball player who represented Australia at the 2012 Summer Paralympics in London, where she won a silver medal.
Diagnosed with chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy at the age of nineteen, Del Toso started playing wheelchair basketball in 2006.
Playing in the local Victorian competition, she was named the league's most valuable player in 2007.
That year started playing for the Knox Ford Raiders in the Women's National Wheelchair Basketball League (WNWBL).
The following year, she was named the team's Players' Player and Most Valuable Player (MVP).
Kevin Wayne Durant (born September 29, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Golden State Warriors of the National Basketball Association (NBA).

He has won an NBA championship, an NBA Most Valuable Player Award, the Bill Russell NBA Finals Most Valuable Player Award, the NBA All-Star Game Most Valuable Player Award, four NBA scoring titles, the NBA Rookie of the Year Award, and two Olympic gold medals.
Durant has also been selected to seven All-NBA teams and eight NBA All-Star teams.
The Chinese Basketball Association (CBA) MVP is the annual award that is handed out at the end of each Chinese Basketball Association (CBA) regular season to the league's most valuable player.
From the league's inaugural 1995–96 season until the 2011–12 season, only Chinese/Taiwanese players were eligible to win the award.
Since the 2012–13 season, separate awards for both domestic and international players are handed out.
Domestic players are eligible to win the CBA Domestic Most Valuable Player award, while international players are eligible to win the CBA International Most Valuable Player award.
Ovinton J'Anthony "O. J." Mayo (born November 5, 1987) is an American professional basketball player.
He played one season of college basketball for the USC Trojans while earning first-team All-Pac-10 honors.
The team forfeited all of its wins that season and Mayo lost his remaining three years of National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) eligibility after it was ruled he received improper benefits.
Mayo entered the 2008 NBA draft and was selected by the Minnesota Timberwolves with the third overall pick.
He was later traded to the Memphis Grizzlies, with whom he played four seasons.
In 2011, he tested positive for a banned steroid and was suspended by the NBA for 10 games.
Mayo signed with the Dallas Mavericks in 2012, and then with the Milwaukee Bucks in 2013.
On July 1, 2016, Mayo was dismissed from the NBA for violating the league's anti-drug program.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

The champion of the tournament was Southern California, who defeated Southern Illinois in the Championship Game.  The Most Valuable Player was O. J. Mayo of Southern California.

2013 NBA Summer League | The 2013 NBA Summer League is a pro basketball league run by the NBA just after the 2013 NBA draft.  It gives newly drafted players a chance to test their skills against each other, and to give them a feel for professional basketball.  All 30 NBA teams participated, along with the D-League Select.  The Miami Heat were the only team to participate in both Summer Leagues.  It ran from July 7–12 in Orlando and July 12–22 in Las Vegas.  Jeremy Lamb of the Oklahoma City Thunder was named the Most Valuable Player of the Orlando Summer League.  Jonas Valančiūnas of the Toronto Raptors went on to be named the Most Valuable Player of the Las Vegas Summer League.  Ian Clark of the Golden State Warriors was named the Most Valuable Player of the Las Vegas Summer League Championship Game.

Kevin Durant | Kevin Wayne Durant (born September 29, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Golden State Warriors of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  He has won an NBA championship, an NBA Most Valuable Player Award, the Bill Russell NBA Finals Most Valuable Player Award, the NBA All-Star Game Most Valuable Player Award, four NBA scoring titles, the NBA Rookie of the Year Award, and two Olympic gold medals.  Durant has also been selected to seven All-NBA teams and eight NBA All-Star teams.

NBA Development League Finals Most Valuable Player Award | NBA Development League Finals Most Valuable Player Award

O. J. Mayo | Ovinton J'Anthony "O. J." Mayo (born November 5, 1987) is an American professional basketball player.  He played one season of college basketball for the USC Trojans while earning first-team All-Pac-10 honors.  The team forfeited all of its wins that season and Mayo lost his remaining three years of National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) eligibility after it was ruled he received improper benefits.  Mayo entered the 2008 NBA draft and was selected by the Minnesota Timberwolves with the third overall pick.  He was later traded to the Memphis Grizzlies, with whom he played four seasons.  In 2011, he tested positive for a banned steroid and was suspended by the NBA for 10 games.  Mayo signed with the Dallas Mavericks in 2012, and then with the Milwaukee Bucks in 2013.  On July 1, 2016, Mayo was dismissed from the NBA for violating the league's anti-drug program.

He is a six-time NBA All-Star, and a two-time NBA All-Star Game Most Valuable Player, winning consecutive awards in 2015 and 2016.  He is also a six-time All-NBA Team member and led the league in scoring in 2014–15 and 2016–17.  In 2017, Westbrook became one of two players in NBA history to average a triple-double for a season, along with Oscar Robertson in 1962.  He also set a record for the most triple-doubles in a season, with 42.  He was subsequently named the 2016–17 NBA Most Valuable Player.

Mayo's full biography (e.g., his Wikipedia page) to confirm the dismissal wording and any cited sources; 2) fetch primary reports or the NBA's announcement using a query like "O. J. Mayo dismissed July 1 2016 'anti-drug program' site:nba.com OR site:espn.com OR site:wikipedia.org" to obtain specifics. [[ ## completed ## ]]
response.hop2_query:
O.J. Mayo banned July 1 2016 "anti-drug program" site:nba.com OR site:espn.com OR site:wikipedia.org OR site:apnews.com OR site:si.com OR site:nytimes.com

hop2_docs: Pro Plus (company) | Pro Plus is a Slovenian multimedia company (d.o.o.) for television management, television productions, films of international studios and sales of television advertising time.  Pro Plus operates the commercial television programs Pop TV, Kanal A, Brio, Oto and Kino, the media web site 24ur. com, the specialized web sites Zadovoljna.si, Bibaleze.si, Cekin.si, Vizita.si, Frendi and Flirt, Moškisvet.com, Okusno.je and Dominvrt.si.

D3hoops.com | D3hoops.com is a website that covers men's and women's NCAA Division III college basketball.  D3hoops.com is most noted for its Top 25 poll of Division III schools, featured on the website of the NCAA.  The site is run by Patrick Coleman and has been featured in Sports Illustrated, ESPN Magazine, the Wall Street Journal, CSTV, and ESPN.  The site has been online since 1997.

Review site | A review site is a website on which reviews can be posted about people, businesses, products, or services.  These sites may use Web 2.0 techniques to gather reviews from site users or may employ professional writers to author reviews on the topic of concern for the site.  Early review sites included first ConsumerDemocracy.com which introduced the helpfulness ratings, Hithen later Epinions.com and Amazon.com.

Starwave | Starwave was a Seattle, Washington based software and website company, founded in 1993 by Paul Allen, co-founder of Microsoft and led by CEO Mike Slade.  The company produced many original CD-ROM titles, including titles for Clint Eastwood, Sting, and Peter Gabriel.  They were the original developers of Castle Infinity, the first MMORPG for kids, but Starwave's most lasting mark was in the area of web content sites.  They developed ESPN.com, ABCNEWS.com, Outside Online, and Mr. Showbiz.com among other groundbreaking sites, setting the standard for much of the commercial Internet explosion of the late 1990s.  Starwave also developed the first site and publishing system for Jim Cramer's TheStreet.com.

TheSabre | TheSabre.com is an independent online publication covering University of Virginia Cavaliers athletics.  The site, originally named "VirginiaFootball.com", was established by Charlottesville, Virginia resident and former U.S. Air Force Security Specialist Michael Ingalls the fall of 1996.  The site changed its name to "TheSabre.com" in 1998 because the site moved into more than just football and basketball coverage.  The change also facilitated greater cooperation by the University of Virginia athletic department as there was some concern over trademark issues - the site held two domain names at the time; previously mentioned "VirginiaFootball.com" as well as "VirginiaBasketball.com".

TrueHoop | TrueHoop is a blog which covers the sport of basketball, primarily the National Basketball Association.  The blog, which has been in operation since May 15, 2005, was one of several blogs honored with the 2005 "Best of the Web award" issued by "Forbes" magazine The site has linked to or been referenced by many notable publications and organizations, including NBA.com, LATimes.com, and CNN/SI. com, and has been praised by notable sports columnists such as Chad Ford and Chris Sheridan.  The site has been noted for its investigations into the affairs of William Wesley, a noted (and somewhat controversial) NBA facilitator.

Gay.com | Gay.com is a chat, personals, and social networking website catering to the LGBT community.  The site is a digital brand of Here Media Inc. In addition to community features, the site features LGBT-related news and features.  As of September 2005, "San Jose Mercury News" ranked gay.com as the most popular online gay personals site in the United States.  As of March 2007, Hitwise ranked it number three in domestic American popularity after Adam4Adam and Manhunt.  Gay.com also competes internationally with dudesnude, gayromeo, and gaydar.
response.summary_2: O. J. Mayo was dismissed from the NBA for violating the league's anti‑drug program. The provided passages do not specify which substance(s) were involved or give the NBA's detailed findings or official ruling.
